definitions: Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation (TENS) is the application of low-frequency current in the form of pulsed rectangular currents through surface electrodes placed on the patient's skin to modulate and reduce pain.
It is a highly popular, non-invasive electrotherapeutic modality widely used by physiotherapists to manage both acute and chronic pain syndromes.
coreConcepts: TENS acts on the underlying neurophysiological principles of the Pain Gate Control Theory and the Endogenous Opiate Release System.

High-frequency TENS stimulates large-diameter A-beta sensory fibers to close the pain gate at the spinal cord (substantia gelatinosa), blocking slow-pain inputs from A-delta and C fibers (presynaptic inhibition).
Low-frequency, high-intensity TENS stimulates small-diameter afferents to induce the release of enkephalins and beta-endorphins within the central nervous system.
It avoids tissue accommodation by utilizing various modulation modes such as frequency, duration, or amplitude sweep.
equipment: TENS pulse generator (portable or clinical stimulator)
Electrode coupling gel (if using carbon rubber)
Saline water and cotton for skin cleaning

pearls: High-frequency TENS acts immediately but has brief carry-over; it is ideal for active movement or exercise preparation.
Low-frequency, high-intensity TENS acts slowly (20–30 mins onset) but provides long-lasting pain relief via systemic endorphin release.
Although clinically most units use microsecond pulse durations, the supreme J. Singh textbook explicitly lists TENS pulse widths as 100 to 500 ms; memorize this for exams.
Placement of electrodes directly over the site of maximum pain is usually the most clinically robust starting option.
Clean skin and good gel coupling are the best safeguards against high skin resistance and patchy current hotspots.
precautions: Always ensure the stimulator's intensity controls are strictly at zero before applying or removing electrodes.
Do not allow the patient to fall asleep during treatment or touch surrounding metal objects.
Inspect the treatment area after session; if mild erythema is present, apply a soothing talcum powder.
Avoid continuous over-treatment to prevent mild chemical or electrolytic reaction below the skin surface.
Do not pull on the electrode leads to avoid damage to wires and contact interfaces.
indications: Reflex sympathetic dystrophy (RSD) / Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS)

general: Patients fitted with cardiac pacemakers (risk of pacemaker frequency interference)
First three months of pregnancy (avoid abdominal and pelvic placement)
local: Placement directly over the carotid sinus (could trigger extreme vasodilation/bradycardia)
Placement directly over or near the eyes
Placement inside the mouth or over mucous membranes
Placement directly over open wounds, cuts, or damaged skin
Placement over thrombophlebitis or venous thrombosis areas

patientPreparation: Greet the patient, introduce yourself, and establish rapport.
Check the patient's case sheet and verify treatment instructions.
Explain the exact procedure and the mild sensory sensations they should expect to experience.
Screen the patient for critical general and local contraindications (such as a cardiac pacemaker, cardiac disease, or early pregnancy).
Position the patient comfortably, fully supported, so that both the painful area and the stimulator controls are easily accessible.
apparatusPreparation: Check the stimulator, ensuring the battery is fully charged.
Inspect lead wires and connections to ensure there is no insulation damage.
Verify that all intensity control knobs are set strictly to zero before switching on the machine.
Perform a brief self-test of the unit on your own palm or forearm to verify normal output.
skinPreparation: Inspect the skin over the treatment area for open wounds, rashes, or local hypersensitivity.
Clean the treatment area using saline water or mild soap and cotton to remove dirt, sebum, and sweat, reducing skin impedance.
Gently pat the skin dry with a clean towel.
electrodePlacement: Identify the area of greater intensity of pain and position the active electrodes directly over or around it.
Alternatively, place electrodes over a superficial nerve trunk proximal to the site of pain.
Position electrodes over the dermatome corresponding to the spinal segment supplying the painful zone.
Or place electrodes over the nerve trunk trigger points that map directly to the painful pain fields.
treatmentProcedure: Securely connect the electrode leads to the stimulator jacks.
Switch on the stimulator and select the appropriate mode: High TENS, Low TENS, or Burst TENS according to the patient file.
Gently and gradually increase the intensity control from zero, communicating with the patient continuously.
For High TENS, increase until the patient reports a mild, comfortable prickling warmth without muscle twitching (typically 12–30 mA).
For Low TENS, elevate the current until a visible, comfortable muscle contraction or twitch is observed (typically 30 mA or more).
Administer the treatment for 40 minutes, checking on the patient regularly to adjust for accommodation.
At the end of the session, slowly reduce the intensity control back to zero, turn off the unit, gently remove the electrodes, and inspect the skin for any localized irritation.

protocols.position: Prone lying, supported with pillows under the abdomen
protocols.electrodePlacement: Four electrodes placed over the paraspinal muscles around the site of lumbar pain
protocols.purpose: Sensory pain gating to facilitate daily movements

protocols.position: Comfortable side-lying or supported supine lying
protocols.electrodePlacement: Parallel to the surgical incision line, at least 2 cm away from the wound margins
protocols.purpose: Inhibit acute incisional pain inputs and reduce reliance on pharmacological analgesics

protocols.electrodePlacement: Placed over the proximal nerve trunk of the amputated limb or contralateral intact limb
protocols.purpose: Stimulate deep neuromodulatory pathways and trigger endogenous opiate release

mechanism: Excitation of low-threshold, large-diameter mechanosensitive A-beta myelinated fibers.
Increased inhibitory interneuron activity in the substantia gelatinosa (SG) of the spinal dorsal horn.
Blocking of transmission cells (T cells), preventing nociceptive inputs from A-delta and C fibers from traveling up the lateral spinothalamic tract to the cerebral cortex.
In Low TENS, high-intensity pulses activate small-diameter motor and nociceptive afferents, which project to the midbrain.
Triggers interaction in the periaqueductal gray (PAG) matter and raphe nucleus of the medulla.
Induces the release of opiate-like chemical substances such as enkephalins and beta-endorphins at the spinal cord level to block descending pain transmission.
Burst TENS consists of high frequency trains repeated at a low rate of 1–5 pulses/sec.
Each train or burst acts for approximately 70 ms.
Combines the immediate pain-gating action of conventional TENS with the sustained opioid-release action of low-frequency acupuncture TENS.

mcqs.question: What is the primary clinical objective of Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation (TENS)?
mcqs.explanation: TENS is designed to satisfy low-frequency pain modulation via sensory and motor level stimulation.

mcqs.question: Which neurophysiological theory explains the mechanism of conventional High TENS?
mcqs.explanation: Conventional High TENS relies on the Pain Gate Control Theory proposed by Melzack and Wall.

mcqs.question: The Pain Gate Control Theory was first proposed in which year?
mcqs.explanation: The pain gate theory was first postulated by Ron Melzack and Pat Wall in 1965.

mcqs.question: In which year did Melzack and Wall modify their famous pain gate theory?
mcqs.explanation: Melzack and Wall further modified the pain gate control theory in 1982.

mcqs.question: Where does the synapse and pain gate mechanism operate in the spinal cord?
options: Substantia gelatinosa of the dorsal horn
mcqs.answer: Substantia gelatinosa of the dorsal horn
mcqs.explanation: The pain gate operates in the substantia gelatinosa and nucleus proprius of the posterior dorsal horn.

mcqs.question: Which specific nerve fibers are stimulated by High TENS to close the pain gate?
mcqs.explanation: High TENS selectively activates large-diameter, low-threshold A-beta myelinated sensory fibers.

mcqs.question: What is the characteristic conduction velocity of A-delta fibers as stated in the textbook?
mcqs.explanation: The textbook states A-delta fibers conduct fast pain with a velocity of 5–30 m/s.

mcqs.question: What is the characteristic conduction velocity of slow C-fibers according to the textbook?
mcqs.explanation: Slow-conducting, non-myelinated C-fibers conduct slow pain at 2–5 m/s.

mcqs.question: Which frequency parameter is designated for High TENS?
mcqs.explanation: Conventional or High TENS utilizes high-frequency stimulation between 100 and 150 Hz.

mcqs.question: Which of the following describes the pulse width parameter range of High TENS in the source?
mcqs.explanation: The Jagmohan Singh Textbook of Electrotherapy explicitly identifies TENS pulse widths as 100 and 500 ms.

mcqs.question: What level of current intensity is typically used in conventional High TENS?
mcqs.explanation: The sensory warmth of conventional High TENS typically requires an intensity range of 12–30 mA.

mcqs.question: Acupuncture-like Low TENS operates in which frequency range?
mcqs.explanation: Acupuncture-like Low TENS is configured with low frequencies of 1–5 Hz.

mcqs.question: What level of intensity is typical for Low TENS to stimulate descending opiate pathways?
mcqs.explanation: Low TENS uses a high current intensity of 30 mA or more, sufficient to trigger opioid release.

mcqs.question: Which descending neurotransmitters are released at the spinal level following Low TENS?
mcqs.explanation: Low frequency high intensity stimulation triggers the release of central opiate-like enkephalins and endorphins.

mcqs.question: Which type of TENS consists of high frequency trains repeated at a low rate of 1–5 times per second?
mcqs.explanation: Burst TENS is a series of impulse trains repeated 1–5 times per second.

mcqs.question: In Burst TENS, each individual impulse train (burst) lasts for how long?
mcqs.explanation: Each train of Burst TENS has a duration of approximately 70 ms.

mcqs.question: TENS units utilize which primary current waveform?
mcqs.explanation: The textbook defines TENS as low frequency current delivered in pulsed rectangular currents.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a classic clinical indication for TENS treatment?
mcqs.explanation: The source identifies phantom limb pain, chronic pain, RSD, and obstetric/postop pain as indications.

mcqs.question: Placement of TENS electrodes directly over which structure is strictly contraindicated?
mcqs.explanation: Direct stimulation of the carotid sinus can disturb baroreceptors, presenting extreme cardiac risks.

mcqs.question: TENS is absolutely contraindicated in patients with which implanted device?
mcqs.explanation: TENS waveforms can interfere with the signaling frequency of cardiac pacemakers.

mcqs.question: During pregnancy, TENS must be strictly avoided during which period?
mcqs.explanation: TENS therapy should be avoided in the first three months of pregnancy.

mcqs.question: Continuous application of High TENS for prolonged durations can cause:
mcqs.explanation: Prolonged continuous application can create undesirable electrolytic changes below the electrodes.

mcqs.question: Which process describes the natural adaptation of a nerve membrane to a constant electrical current?
mcqs.explanation: Accommodation is the physiological adaptation of nerve tissue to a stable, unchanged electrical field.

mcqs.question: For a single daily TENS treatment, what is the standard recommended duration?
mcqs.explanation: The textbook states that TENS can be used for a single daily treatment of 40 minutes duration.

mcqs.question: What is the maximum duration in a 24-hour cycle that portable TENS units can be utilized continuously?
mcqs.explanation: Portable TENS units can be worn and used continuously for up to 24 hours.

mcqs.question: If mild erythema occurs on the skin following TENS therapy, what is the best immediate step?
mcqs.explanation: If mild skin redness occurs from TENS, applying a soothing talcum powder is recommended.

mcqs.question: Prior to treatment, skin prep is done with saline or soap to reduce what property?
mcqs.explanation: Washing the skin removes sebum and sweat, lowering high skin resistance to facilitate smooth current flow.

mcqs.question: The state where the nerve is positive outside and negative inside is called:
mcqs.explanation: In a relaxed state without active firing, a healthy resting nerve remains positive outside and negative inside.

mcqs.question: Which of the following of TENS electrode placements targets corresponding spinal sensory segments?
mcqs.explanation: Dermatomal placement routes stimulation along corresponding segmental spinal nerve roots.

mcqs.question: The therapeutic benefits of Burst TENS are derived from:
options: Both gate control and opioid release systems
mcqs.answer: Both gate control and opioid release systems
mcqs.explanation: Burst TENS blends high-frequency trains with low repetition, activating both gating and opiate mechanisms.

viva.question: What is the definition of TENS according to the source?
Explain the physiological rationale behind High TENS.
Describe the physiological mechanism involved in Low TENS.
State the exact parameter ranges associated with High TENS.
What parameters are used when configuring Low TENS?
How does Burst TENS combine properties of both High and Low TENS?
Describe the four primary electrode placements used for TENS treatment.
What are the main indications for using TENS clinically?
Why is TENS absolutely contraindicated in patients with cardiac pacemakers?
Explain why TENS must not be applied directly over the carotid sinus.

references: Jagmohan Singh - Textbook of Electrotherapy - Low Frequency Currents - Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation
Melzack & Wall - Pain Gate Control Theory (1965/1982)

parameters.highTENSPulseWidth: 100 and 500 ms (as specified in textbook source)
parameters.lowTENSPulseWidth: 100 and 500 ms (as specified in textbook source)
parameters.lowTENSIntensity: 30 mA or more (producing clear muscle twitching/sensory contraction)
parameters.treatmentTime: 40 minutes for single daily session, or up to 24 hours continuously using portable units
